result.content:
1: # Lex - Vietnamese Telex input method
2: 
3: The specification of the Lex program, a Vietnamese Telex input method.
4: 
5: ## Vietnamese Telex Rules
6: 
7: ### Literal input characters
8: 
9: - When any of the following characters start the word (when the word is empty), change to literal
10:   input from the new character position: `F`, `J`, `W`, `Z` (ignore cases), because these characters
11:   don't start a Vietnamese word. This rule is higher priority than diacritics and tones rules.
12: - When any of the following characters are added to the word, change to literal input from the new
13:   character position: `F`, `J`, `W` (ignore cases), because these characters don't appear in formal
14:   Vietnamese spelling. `Z` exists in Vietnamese word such as _Dzũng_, _Hồ Dzếnh_... This rule is
15:   lower priority than diacritics and tones rules.
16: - Literal input remains until the new word.
17: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
18: 
19: ### Diacritics
20: 
21: Diacritics let a character have many variant. We only focus on character diacritics in this section.
22: Tones have a separate section.
23: 
24: #### Empty
25: 
26: - Any literal alphabet character has empty diacritic by default.
27: - Only a specific number of characters have other diacritic and it must follow the rules for those
28:   characters.
29: 
30: #### Circumflex
31: 
32: - Valid for: `A` (Â), `E` (Ê), `O` (Ô) and their lower cases.
33: 
34: #### Breve
35: 
36: - Valid for: `A` (Ă) and its lower case.
37: 
38: #### Horn
39: 
40: - Valid for: `O` (Ơ), `U` (Ư) and their lower cases.
41: 
42: #### Stroke
43: 
44: - Valid for: `D` (Đ) and its lower case.
45: 
46: #### Apply rules
47: 
48: - Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty).
49: - _Circumflex_, _stroke_ are triggered when user inputs the same character (ignore case) with the
50:   existing character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic.
51:   - `Aa` -> `Â`, `Ee` -> `Ê`, `Oo` -> `Ô`.
52:   - `Dd` -> `Đ`.
53: - _Breve_, _horn_ are triggered when user input `W`, `w` after the character when that character is
54:   valid for the applying diacritic. Otherwise, treat `W`, `w` literally.
55:   - `Aw` -> `Ă`.
56:   - `Ow` -> `Ơ`, `Uw` -> `Ư`.
57: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
58: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
59: 
60: #### Override rules
61: 
62: - New valid diacritic can override existing valid diacritic of the same base character.
63:   - `Ăa` -> `Â`, `Âw` -> `Ă`.
64:   - `Ôw` -> `Ơ`, `Ơo` -> `Ô`.
65: - Only `A`, `O` have override rules because they have more than one possible character diacritic.
66: - Keep tone untouched.
67: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
68: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
69: 
70: #### Cancel rules
71: 
72: - The cancellation is triggered if and only if user inputs the character (ignore case) that would
73:   produce the existing diacritic for the same base character of the immediate previous character.
74: - The existing character keeps its case, tone, but the diacritic is removed.
75:   - Circumflex: `Âa` -> `Aa`, `Ốo` -> `Óo`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
76:   - Breve: `Ăw` -> `Aw`.
77:   - Horn: `Ưw` -> `Uw`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
78:   - Stroke: `Đd` -> `Dd`, `đD` -> `dD`.
79: - The new character is literal, keeps its case, no tone.
80: - Switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters. Literal input
81:   remains until the new word.
82:   - Existing `Â`, input `aa`, result `Aaa`.
83: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
84: 
85: #### Auto-fill rules
86: 
87: - The auto-fill is only applied for `ươ` syllable immediately before the user input, when one of the
88:   character in the syllable is missing horn: `uơ` and `ưo`.
89: - Fill the missing _horn_ when the input is one of these character (ignore case): `C`, `I`, `M`,
90:   `N`, `P`, `T`, `U` and their lower cases.
91:   - Fill missing horn on `U`: `uơn` -> `ươn`.
92:   - Fill missing horn on `O`: `ưóp` -> `ướp`.
93: - The new input character is appended literally.
94: - Tone and case of existing characters are preserved on the original characters.
95: 
96: ### Tones
97: 
98: Tones let a word have many variants, each variant has different meaning. Only the following
99: Vietnamese vowel could have tones: `A`, `E`, `I`, `O`, `U`, `Y` and their corresponding variants.
100: These vowel could take any tone.
101: 
102: #### Level (ngang)
103: 
104: - The default tone, it's the vowel without any tone mark, e.g. `A`, `Ê`, `Ơ`...and all other
105:   corresponding variants.
106: - If a vowel has tone mark other than level, use `Z`, `z` to reset the tone to level.
107: 
108: #### Rising (sắc)
109: 
110: - Example of rising tone: `Á`, `Ế`, `Ớ`...and all other corresponding variants.
111: - Use `S`, `s` to put the rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `As` -> `Á`, `Ês` -> `Ế`, `Ơs`
112:   -> `Ớ`.
113: 
114: #### Falling (huyền)
115: 
116: - Example of falling tone: `À`, `Ề`, `Ờ`...and all other corresponding variants.
117: - Use `F`, `f` to put the falling tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Af` -> `À`, `Êf` -> `Ề`, `Ơf`
118:   -> `Ờ`.
119: 
120: #### Dipping-rising (hỏi)
121: 
122: - Example of dipping-rising tone: `Ả`, `Ể`, `Ở`...and all other corresponding variants.
123: - Use `R`, `r` to put the dipping-rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ar` -> `Ả`, `Êr` ->
124:   `Ể`, `Ơr` -> `Ở`.
125: 
126: #### Rising-glottalized (ngã)
127: 
128: - Example of rising-glottalized tone: `Ã`, `Ễ`, `Ỡ`...and all other corresponding variants.
129: - Use `X`, `x` to put the rising-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ax` -> `Ã`, `Êx`
130:   -> `Ễ`, `Ơx` -> `Ỡ`.
131: 
132: #### Falling-glottalized (nặng)
133: 
134: - Example of falling-glottalized tone: `Ạ`, `Ệ`, `Ợ`...and all other corresponding variants.
135: - Use `J`, `j` to put the falling-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Aj` -> `Ạ`, `Êj`
136:   -> `Ệ`, `Ơj` -> `Ợ`.
137: 
138: #### Apply rules
139: 
140: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
141:   character literally.
142: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
143:   their lower cases.
144: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
145: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append new character literally.
146: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and all of them don't have any tone (level), use _tone
147:   position rules_ to apply the tone for the word.
148: - If _tone position rules_ could not put tone to the pseudo-word, append new character literally.
149: 
150: #### Cancel rules
151: 
152: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
153:   character literally.
154: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
155:   their lower cases.
156: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
157: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
158: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels match with
159:   the triggered tone. Reset that tone to level. Append new character literally.
160: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
161:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
162: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
163: 
164: #### Reset rules
165: 
166: - Only trigger reset tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
167:   character literally.
168: - Trigger reset tone when user inputs the trigger character for reset: `Z`, `z`.
169: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
170: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has tone (other than level) in one of the vowels,
171:   change that tone to level, keep case and diacritic.
172: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels or has at least one vowel and all of them don't have
173:   any tone (level), append the new character literally.
174: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
175:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
176: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
177: 
178: #### Override rules
179: 
180: - Only trigger override tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
181:   character literally.
182: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
183:   their lower cases.
184: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
185: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
186: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels different
187:   from the triggered tone, reset that tone to level, use _tone position rules_ to apply new tone for
188:   the word. The override tone is always successful.
189: 
190: #### Scan pseudo-word
191: 
192: - Scan pseudo-word is a process to get the information needed from the existing characters to put
193:   the tone in the correct position.
194: - The existing characters must not be empty.
195: - Iterate backward from the last existing character (the new input is the tone trigger character).
196: - If a vowel is found first, continue go backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no
197:   character to scan, then stop.
198: - If a consonant is found first, continue go backward until found the first vowel, then continue go
199:   backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no character to scan, then stop.
200: - After iterated, a context have been formed either (in the left to right order, not the reverse)
201:   `<consonants>`, `<vowels><consonants>`, `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`, `<vowels>` or
202:   `<consonant><vowels>`.
203: 
204: #### Tone position rules
205: 
206: The process of applying tone to a word go through many steps:
207: 
208: - Focus on the `<vowels>` in peusdo-word, it could be one vowel but could be multiple vowels, the
209:   tone will be applied on one of these vowels.
210: - Exceptions:
211:   - Word exactly `QU` (and their lower cases), could not put tone, this is a consonant only.
212: - If one vowel, put the tone on that vowel.
213: - If multiple vowels, follow these rules, priority from top down:
214:   - Vowels has `Ơ` (and its lower case), put on the rightmost `Ơ`.
215:   - Vowels has one any of `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư` (and their lower cases), put on the rightmost
216:     listed vowel found by the reverse scan.
217:   - Word start with `GI`, `QU` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels, put on next vowel, `I` /
218:     `U` count as part of the consonant.
219:   - Vowels exactly `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases), put on first vowel.
220:   - Vowels start with `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels or
221:     consonants, put on second vowel.
222:   - Otherwise, put on first vowel.
223: - Preserve case and diacritic on existing vowels.
224: - Could not put tone for other cases.

I see the spec has shifted significantly. The literal input mode now triggers when F, J, W, or Z start a word with an empty buffer, and this takes priority over diacritics and tones rules. There's a second rule forming around F, J, W but it's cut off. So the spec change splits the literal input rule: empty buffer gets F, J, W, Z with high priority, while non-empty buffer only gets F, J, W with lower priority, leaving Z to be handled by tone reset rules instead. I need to update the tests accordingly — adding Z and z variants to the empty buffer test, and checking whether existing tests for Z on non-empty buffers need adjustment or if new tests are needed to cover Z's behavior under the reset rules.

Let me search for Z and z test cases to see what's currently covered.
I should check how the production code handles uppercase and lowercase 'Z' characters, and look at any tests related to tone resets.
I'm checking if there are any tests that cover the Z key behavior for resetting or clearing the level, whether the buffer is empty or has content.
I need to find where literal_index is being used in the test suite.

I'm clarifying the spec change for how literal mode gets triggered. The old rule treated F, J, W, and Z the same way—adding any of them would switch to literal mode with lower priority than diacritics and tones. The new approach splits this into two cases: when the buffer is empty at the start of a word, F, J, W, and Z now trigger literal mode with higher priority, but the behavior changes when the buffer already has content.

On a non-empty buffer, only F, J, and W switch to literal mode with lower priority than diacritics and tones. Z gets removed from this rule because it appears in valid Vietnamese words like Dzũng and Hồ Dzếnh. Instead, Z is now governed by the tone-reset rules—it acts as a level-tone reset trigger, and when it can't reset, it appends literally and switches to literal mode.

Looking at the test coverage, I need to update the test at lex.zig:2020 to include Z and z cases alongside the existing F, J, W tests, and update the title to reflect that Z is now included. The other tests remain valid since the spec change doesn't alter F and J behavior on non-empty buffers, and Z was correctly excluded from those tests before. The new spec does create an explicit gap worth covering positively—confirming that Z on an empty buffer doesn't attempt a tone reset, which is already structurally covered since reset rules require a non-empty buffer. I should get a second opinion on this approach before finalizing the test changes.

content.text:
I have a clear picture of the spec change and what's already in the test file. Here's my proposed approach.

## Spec change summary

The single bullet "F, J, W, Z added → literal" was split into two rules:

1. **Empty buffer (word start):** `F`, `J`, `W`, **`Z`** trigger literal mode. **Higher** priority than diacritics/tones.
2. **Non-empty buffer:** `F`, `J`, `W` (no `Z`) trigger literal mode. **Lower** priority than diacritics/tones.

`Z` is intentionally removed from the non-empty rule because it can appear in valid Vietnamese words (Dzũng, Hồ Dzếnh), and on non-empty buffers it is governed by the **Tones — Reset rules** instead.

## Proposed test changes (only to tests in [lex.zig](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/lex.zig))

1. **[lex.zig:2020](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/lex.zig#L2020)** – "switch to literal … on empty buffer":
   - Rename the test title to include `Z`.
   - Extend the `inputs` array from `{F,f,J,j,W,w}` to also include `Z, z`.
   - Existing assertions (length 1, literal_index 0, mode .telex, span empty/level) remain identical.

2. **[lex.zig:2045](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/lex.zig#L2045)** – "switch to literal … W (ignore cases) on character outside diacritic scope": no change. W on non-empty buffer still triggers literal.

3. **[lex.zig:2095](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/lex.zig#L2095)** – "switch to literal … F, J (ignore cases) on word without placeable tone": no change. Z was never asserted here and must stay absent under the new spec.

4. No new tests needed for "Z on non-empty buffer doesn't force literal" because Z's non-empty behavior is already exercised by the tone-reset rule tests (and there is currently no test asserting the old behavior, so nothing to delete).

Now consulting the Oracle for another perspective.

input.task:
Review my plan for adjusting tests to a spec change. Provide critique only — do NOT write or include any example code (no Zig snippets, no diff snippets, no pseudo-code). Plain prose only.

Spec change (Vietnamese Telex input method, "Literal input characters" section):
Old rule (single bullet): When F, J, W, or Z (any case) is added to the word, switch to literal input from the new character position. Lower priority than diacritics/tones rules.
New rules (two bullets):
1. When the word is empty and the user inputs F, J, W, or Z (any case), switch to literal input. HIGHER priority than diacritics and tones rules.
2. When the word is non-empty and the user inputs F, J, or W (any case — note Z is excluded), switch to literal input. LOWER priority than diacritics and tones rules.
Rationale for excluding Z in rule 2: Z can appear in valid Vietnamese words like Dzũng and Hồ Dzếnh. On non-empty buffers Z behavior is governed by the Tones — Reset rules.

Existing tests in src/lex.zig that touch this area:
- Test at line 2020: "expect State.add switch to literal input when append F, J, W (ignore cases) on empty buffer" — iterates F, f, J, j, W, w on an empty buffer, asserts buffer_length=1, literal_index=0, mode=.telex, span has empty diacritic and level tone.
- Test at line 2045: "expect State.add switch to literal input when append W (ignore cases) on character outside diacritic scope" — non-empty buffer with W input, asserts literal_index=1.
- Test at line 2095: "expect State.add switch to literal input when append F, J (ignore cases) on word without placeable tone" — non-empty buffer with F/J input, two sub-blocks (single consonant, multi-consonant + QU), asserts literal_index after the new character.
- Other tone/diacritic tests exist that may exercise Z via reset rules.

My proposed test changes (only tests, never production code):
1. lex.zig:2020 — extend the inputs array to add Z and z; rename the test title to include Z. Keep all assertions identical.
2. lex.zig:2045 — no change. W on non-empty still triggers literal.
3. lex.zig:2095 — no change. Z was never in this test and must stay out under the new spec.
4. No new tests added for "Z on non-empty buffer does not force literal" because Z behavior on non-empty buffer is already exercised by tone-reset tests, and no current test asserts the old (incorrect) behavior.

Please critique:
- Is this plan complete to fully cover the new spec for the Literal input characters section?
- Any gaps or risks in coverage I am missing?
- Any tests I might be wrongly leaving alone that the spec change actually affects?
- Should the empty-buffer Z case have any additional assertion beyond what F/J/W already check (e.g., to evidence the new "higher priority than diacritics/tones" wording)?
- Any redundancy or naming concerns?

Respond with prose-only critique. Do not output code, diffs, or pseudo-code in any form.

run.result:
1. TL;DR

Your plan is mostly right, but I would not call it fully complete. Adding empty-buffer Z to the existing empty-buffer literal test is necessary, and leaving the W and F/J fallback tests alone is reasonable; the main gap is that you are relying on non-empty Z reset coverage that does not appear to be explicit in this file.

2. Recommended approach

1. Keep the change to the empty-buffer test at line 2020. That is the clearest direct response to the spec delta.
2. Keep line 2045 unchanged. It still matches the non-empty W fallback case.
3. Keep line 2095 unchanged, and in fact its continued exclusion of Z is now desirable because it documents the spec split.
4. Add one explicit non-empty Z regression under the tone-reset area, not under the literal-input area. Scope is small, roughly S. The important case is a non-empty word where Z should be handled by reset logic rather than generic literal-input logic.
5. If your goal is truly “full coverage of the Literal input characters section,” verify that the suite already has at least one representative non-empty F or J case where tone handling wins over literal fallback. I do not see that clearly in the visible tests.

3. Rationale and trade-offs

The strong part of your plan is that it captures the only obviously new empty-buffer character: Z. Your existing assertions are already the right ones for proving the new higher-priority empty-buffer behavior. In particular, the literal index being set at the first character is the important evidence; that is what distinguishes “switch to literal input” from merely “append literally.”

The weak part is step 4. The new spec does not merely say “Z is absent from one list”; it says non-empty Z is governed by tone reset rules. That deserves at least one direct test somewhere. Otherwise you have a documentation gap in the suite, and possibly a real coverage gap too.

Also, be careful with the phrasing “Z on non-empty buffer does not force literal.” That statement is too broad. On some non-empty inputs, Z still can end up appended literally and start literal mode via reset-rule fallback. What changed is which rule family owns that behavior, not that Z can never become literal once the buffer is non-empty. So if you add or review naming around this, the wording should reflect “handled by reset rules” rather than “never literal.”

4. Risks and guardrails

The biggest risk is false confidence from assuming existing reset tests already cover Z. In the portion of `src/lex.zig` I reviewed, I do not see explicit Z/reset tests. If they exist elsewhere, great; if not, your current plan leaves the new non-empty Z rule partition largely implicit.

A second risk is that combining Z into the existing empty-buffer F/J/W test may technically work but slightly hides the reason Z is special. That is not wrong, just less communicative. If a failure happens later, it may be less obvious whether the regression is about invalid onsets generally or about Z’s new empty-buffer priority.

A smaller risk is completeness drift: W is already well represented on the “higher-priority diacritic beats literal fallback” side, but F and J do not appear to have equally visible representative “tone beats literal fallback” tests in the file. That is not caused by this spec change, but it matters if you are aiming for complete section-level coverage rather than just a targeted patch.

5. When to consider the advanced path

Consider a more systematic test expansion only if one of these is true:

- You want the suite to serve as a spec matrix, not just a regression net.
- You are about to refactor input dispatch or priority ordering.
- You have had regressions where a trigger character was routed to the wrong rule family.
- You want every trigger named in the spec to have both a fallback case and a priority-wins case.

6. Optional advanced path

If you do go beyond the minimal patch, the clean structure is a small rule-ownership matrix: empty-buffer literal-start cases, non-empty fallback-to-literal cases, and non-empty higher-priority handling cases. In that framing, Z belongs with reset tests on non-empty buffers, not with the literal-input fallback tests.

Net assessment: your plan is a good minimal start, but not quite complete. The empty-buffer Z addition is correct; the missing piece is one direct non-empty Z reset-oriented regression, plus a quick sanity check that F/J priority-over-tone behavior is represented somewhere if you truly want full section coverage.

42: #### Stroke
43: 
44: - Valid for: `D` (Đ) and its lower case.
45: 
46: #### Apply rules
47: 
48: - Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty).
49: - _Circumflex_, _stroke_ are triggered when user inputs the same character (ignore case) with the
50:   existing character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic.
51:   - `Aa` -> `Â`, `Ee` -> `Ê`, `Oo` -> `Ô`.
52:   - `Dd` -> `Đ`.
53: - _Breve_, _horn_ are triggered when user input `W`, `w` after the character when that character is
54:   valid for the applying diacritic. Otherwise, treat `W`, `w` literally.
55:   - `Aw` -> `Ă`.
56:   - `Ow` -> `Ơ`, `Uw` -> `Ư`.
57: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
58: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
59: 
60: #### Override rules
61: 
62: - New valid diacritic can override existing valid diacritic of the same base character.
63:   - `Ăa` -> `Â`, `Âw` -> `Ă`.
64:   - `Ôw` -> `Ơ`, `Ơo` -> `Ô`.
65: - Only `A`, `O` have override rules because they have more than one possible character diacritic.
66: - Keep tone untouched.
67: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
68: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
69: 
70: #### Cancel rules
71: 
72: - The cancellation is triggered if and only if user inputs the character (ignore case) that would
73:   produce the existing diacritic for the same base character of the immediate previous character.
74: - The existing character keeps its case, tone, but the diacritic is removed.
75:   - Circumflex: `Âa` -> `Aa`, `Ốo` -> `Óo`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
76:   - Breve: `Ăw` -> `Aw`.
77:   - Horn: `Ưw` -> `Uw`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
78:   - Stroke: `Đd` -> `Dd`, `đD` -> `dD`.
79: - The new character is literal, keeps its case, no tone.
80: - Switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters. Literal input
81:   remains until the new word.
82:   - Existing `Â`, input `aa`, result `Aaa`.
83: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
84: 
85: #### Auto-fill rules
86: 
87: - The auto-fill is only applied for `ươ` syllable immediately before the user input, when one of the
88:   character in the syllable is missing horn: `uơ` and `ưo`.
89: - Fill the missing _horn_ when the input is one of these character (ignore case): `C`, `I`, `M`,
90:   `N`, `P`, `T`, `U` and their lower cases.
91:   - Fill missing horn on `U`: `uơn` -> `ươn`.
92:   - Fill missing horn on `O`: `ưóp` -> `ướp`.
93: - The new input character is appended literally.
94: - Tone and case of existing characters are preserved on the original characters.
95: 
96: ### Tones
97: 
98: Tones let a word have many variants, each variant has different meaning. Only the following
99: Vietnamese vowel could have tones: `A`, `E`, `I`, `O`, `U`, `Y` and their corresponding variants.
100: These vowel could take any tone.
101: 
102: #### Level (ngang)
103: 
104: - The default tone, it's the vowel without any tone mark, e.g. `A`, `Ê`, `Ơ`...and all other
105:   corresponding variants.
106: - If a vowel has tone mark other than level, use `Z`, `z` to reset the tone to level.
107: 
108: #### Rising (sắc)
109: 
110: - Example of rising tone: `Á`, `Ế`, `Ớ`...and all other corresponding variants.
111: - Use `S`, `s` to put the rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `As` -> `Á`, `Ês` -> `Ế`, `Ơs`
112:   -> `Ớ`.
113: 
114: #### Falling (huyền)
115: 
116: - Example of falling tone: `À`, `Ề`, `Ờ`...and all other corresponding variants.
117: - Use `F`, `f` to put the falling tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Af` -> `À`, `Êf` -> `Ề`, `Ơf`
118:   -> `Ờ`.
119: 
120: #### Dipping-rising (hỏi)
121: 
122: - Example of dipping-rising tone: `Ả`, `Ể`, `Ở`...and all other corresponding variants.
123: - Use `R`, `r` to put the dipping-rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ar` -> `Ả`, `Êr` ->
124:   `Ể`, `Ơr` -> `Ở`.
125: 
126: #### Rising-glottalized (ngã)
127: 
128: - Example of rising-glottalized tone: `Ã`, `Ễ`, `Ỡ`...and all other corresponding variants.
129: - Use `X`, `x` to put the rising-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ax` -> `Ã`, `Êx`
130:   -> `Ễ`, `Ơx` -> `Ỡ`.
131: 
132: #### Falling-glottalized (nặng)
133: 
134: - Example of falling-glottalized tone: `Ạ`, `Ệ`, `Ợ`...and all other corresponding variants.
135: - Use `J`, `j` to put the falling-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Aj` -> `Ạ`, `Êj`
136:   -> `Ệ`, `Ơj` -> `Ợ`.
137: 
138: #### Apply rules
139: 
140: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
141:   character literally.
142: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
143:   their lower cases.
144: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
145: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append new character literally.
146: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and all of them don't have any tone (level), use _tone
147:   position rules_ to apply the tone for the word.
148: - If _tone position rules_ could not put tone to the pseudo-word, append new character literally.
149: 
150: #### Cancel rules
151: 
152: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
153:   character literally.
154: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
155:   their lower cases.
156: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
157: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
158: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels match with
159:   the triggered tone. Reset that tone to level. Append new character literally.
160: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
161:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
162: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
163: 
164: #### Reset rules
165: 
166: - Only trigger reset tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
167:   character literally.
168: - Trigger reset tone when user inputs the trigger character for reset: `Z`, `z`.
169: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
170: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has tone (other than level) in one of the vowels,
171:   change that tone to level, keep case and diacritic.
172: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels or has at least one vowel and all of them don't have
173:   any tone (level), append the new character literally.
174: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
175:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
176: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
177: 
178: #### Override rules
179: 
180: - Only trigger override tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
181:   character literally.
182: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
183:   their lower cases.
184: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
185: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
186: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels different
187:   from the triggered tone, reset that tone to level, use _tone position rules_ to apply new tone for
188:   the word. The override tone is always successful.
189: 
190: #### Scan pseudo-word
191: 
192: - Scan pseudo-word is a process to get the information needed from the existing characters to put
193:   the tone in the correct position.
194: - The existing characters must not be empty.
195: - Iterate backward from the last existing character (the new input is the tone trigger character).
196: - If a vowel is found first, continue go backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no
197:   character to scan, then stop.
198: - If a consonant is found first, continue go backward until found the first vowel, then continue go
199:   backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no character to scan, then stop.
200: - After iterated, a context have been formed either (in the left to right order, not the reverse)
201:   `<consonants>`, `<vowels><consonants>`, `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`, `<vowels>` or
202:   `<consonant><vowels>`.
203: 
204: #### Tone position rules
205: 
206: The process of applying tone to a word go through many steps:
207: 
208: - Focus on the `<vowels>` in peusdo-word, it could be one vowel but could be multiple vowels, the
209:   tone will be applied on one of these vowels.
210: - Exceptions:
211:   - Word exactly `QU` (and their lower cases), could not put tone, this is a consonant only.
212: - If one vowel, put the tone on that vowel.
213: - If multiple vowels, follow these rules, priority from top down:
214:   - Vowels has `Ơ` (and its lower case), put on the rightmost `Ơ`.
215:   - Vowels has one any of `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư` (and their lower cases), put on the rightmost
216:     listed vowel found by the reverse scan.
217:   - Word start with `GI`, `QU` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels, put on next vowel, `I` /
218:     `U` count as part of the consonant.
219:   - Vowels exactly `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases), put on first vowel.
220:   - Vowels start with `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels or
221:     consonants, put on second vowel.
222:   - Otherwise, put on first vowel.
223: - Preserve case and diacritic on existing vowels.
224: - Could not put tone for other cases.

1. TL;DR

Your plan is mostly right, but I would not call it fully complete. Adding empty-buffer Z to the existing empty-buffer literal test is necessary, and leaving the W and F/J fallback tests alone is reasonable; the main gap is that you are relying on non-empty Z reset coverage that does not appear to be explicit in this file.

2. Recommended approach

1. Keep the change to the empty-buffer test at line 2020. That is the clearest direct response to the spec delta.
2. Keep line 2045 unchanged. It still matches the non-empty W fallback case.
3. Keep line 2095 unchanged, and in fact its continued exclusion of Z is now desirable because it documents the spec split.
4. Add one explicit non-empty Z regression under the tone-reset area, not under the literal-input area. Scope is small, roughly S. The important case is a non-empty word where Z should be handled by reset logic rather than generic literal-input logic.
5. If your goal is truly “full coverage of the Literal input characters section,” verify that the suite already has at least one representative non-empty F or J case where tone handling wins over literal fallback. I do not see that clearly in the visible tests.

3. Rationale and trade-offs

The strong part of your plan is that it captures the only obviously new empty-buffer character: Z. Your existing assertions are already the right ones for proving the new higher-priority empty-buffer behavior. In particular, the literal index being set at the first character is the important evidence; that is what distinguishes “switch to literal input” from merely “append literally.”

The weak part is step 4. The new spec does not merely say “Z is absent from one list”; it says non-empty Z is governed by tone reset rules. That deserves at least one direct test somewhere. Otherwise you have a documentation gap in the suite, and possibly a real coverage gap too.

Also, be careful with the phrasing “Z on non-empty buffer does not force literal.” That statement is too broad. On some non-empty inputs, Z still can end up appended literally and start literal mode via reset-rule fallback. What changed is which rule family owns that behavior, not that Z can never become literal once the buffer is non-empty. So if you add or review naming around this, the wording should reflect “handled by reset rules” rather than “never literal.”

4. Risks and guardrails

The biggest risk is false confidence from assuming existing reset tests already cover Z. In the portion of `src/lex.zig` I reviewed, I do not see explicit Z/reset tests. If they exist elsewhere, great; if not, your current plan leaves the new non-empty Z rule partition largely implicit.

A second risk is that combining Z into the existing empty-buffer F/J/W test may technically work but slightly hides the reason Z is special. That is not wrong, just less communicative. If a failure happens later, it may be less obvious whether the regression is about invalid onsets generally or about Z’s new empty-buffer priority.

A smaller risk is completeness drift: W is already well represented on the “higher-priority diacritic beats literal fallback” side, but F and J do not appear to have equally visible representative “tone beats literal fallback” tests in the file. That is not caused by this spec change, but it matters if you are aiming for complete section-level coverage rather than just a targeted patch.

5. When to consider the advanced path

Consider a more systematic test expansion only if one of these is true:

- You want the suite to serve as a spec matrix, not just a regression net.
- You are about to refactor input dispatch or priority ordering.
- You have had regressions where a trigger character was routed to the wrong rule family.
- You want every trigger named in the spec to have both a fallback case and a priority-wins case.

6. Optional advanced path

If you do go beyond the minimal patch, the clean structure is a small rule-ownership matrix: empty-buffer literal-start cases, non-empty fallback-to-literal cases, and non-empty higher-priority handling cases. In that framing, Z belongs with reset tests on non-empty buffers, not with the literal-input fallback tests.

Net assessment: your plan is a good minimal start, but not quite complete. The empty-buffer Z addition is correct; the missing piece is one direct non-empty Z reset-oriented regression, plus a quick sanity check that F/J priority-over-tone behavior is represented somewhere if you truly want full section coverage.

## Suggested spec change (for you to apply)

In **Tones → Reset rules** ([spec lines 165–176](file:///<redacted>/lex/specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md#L165-L176)), split the current "no-vowel OR all-level-vowels" bullet into two separate outcomes so the no-vowel branch keeps Vietnamese input:

Replace these three bullets:

> - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels or has at least one vowel and all of them don't have any tone (level), append the new character literally.
> - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
> - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.

With:

> - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append the new character literally and remain in Vietnamese input mode (the literal point is not set), so following input continues to be processed as Vietnamese (e.g., `d` + `z` → `dz`, then `dzu` + `x` → `dzũ`).
> - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and all of them don't have any tone (level), append the new character literally and switch to literal from the new character position. Literal input remains until the new word.
> - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.

(No change is needed in **Apply rules** or **Cancel rules** — the no-vowel branch already does not switch to literal in those.)

## Preview of final test plan (only after you update the spec)

All edits are in [lex.zig](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/lex.zig). No production code is touched.

1. **[lex.zig:2020](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/lex.zig#L2020)** — extend `inputs` with `Z`, `z`; rename the title to include `Z`. Existing assertions (`buffer_length=1`, `literal_index=0`, `mode=.telex`, span `empty/level`) already evidence the new "higher priority on empty buffer" wording.

2. **[lex.zig:2045](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/lex.zig#L2045)** — no change.

3. **[lex.zig:2095](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/lex.zig#L2095)** — no change. Z's continued absence here documents the spec split.

4. **New test** — "expect State.add appends Z literally and switches to literal when pseudo-word has vowel(s) all level". Cases like `ba` + `z` → `baz` (literal_index=2), `a` + `z` → `az` (literal_index=1), `qua` + `z` → `quaz` (literal_index=3). Assertions: literal_index points to the new Z, mode .telex, span empty/level.

5. **New test** — "expect State.add appends Z literally and remains Vietnamese when pseudo-word has no vowel". Cases like `d` + `z` → `dz`, `ng` + `z` → `ngz`. Assertions: `literal_index=null`, mode .telex, span empty/level. Optionally a follow-up `add` to demonstrate Vietnamese processing still applies (e.g., `dzu` + `x` → `dzũ`); skip if redundant with existing tone tests.

Open question: do you want test 5 to include the follow-up character (proving Vietnamese mode is preserved), or stop at the bare "literal_index stays null" assertion? My preference is the bare assertion — it's the direct contract of the new spec bullet — and the follow-up adds coupling to apply-tone behavior already covered elsewhere.

Confirm the spec change wording and the test plan, and tell me which variant of test 5 you want before I edit.

- My preference is the bare assertion. -> Agree.

2. **No change** to [lex.zig:2045](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/lex.zig#L2045) (W on non-empty, outside diacritic scope).
